skill.name: writing-great-skills
skill.description: Reference for writing and editing AI skills with predictable invocation, clear structure, useful reference, and careful pruning.
skill.summary: Improve AI skills by making invocation, structure, reference, and pruning decisions explicit.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: AI review found the 57 static detections to be false positives in documentation files. The files contain Markdown links, inline code formatting, and conceptual writing guidance, with no scripts, network calls, command execution, cryptographic operations, or prompt injection attempts.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks and links as Ruby or shell execution. These locations are documentation text and inline formatting, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain Markdown links, frontmatter field names, and inline code references. No shell command, Ruby backtick execution, or command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Prose Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged conceptual documentation as weak cryptography. The reviewed lines describe skill design terms and contain no cryptographic APIs, algorithms, hashing calls, or encryption logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed content is explanatory prose about AI skill design. There is no implementation code, no crypto library use, and no security-sensitive computation.

content.value_statement: AI skills often become vague, bloated, or hard to invoke reliably. This skill gives clear vocabulary and review principles for writing predictable, maintainable skill instructions.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use model-invoked or user-invoked skills.
Defines core terms for skill structure, invocation, and reference design.
Guides authors on descriptions, leading words, and context pointers.
Helps identify duplication, sprawl, sediment, and no-op instructions.
Shows when to split skills by invocation, branch, or sequence.
Provides a glossary for consistent skill design vocabulary.
limitations: It is reference material, not an automated linter.
It does not create or edit files by itself.
It does not validate marketplace schemas or package metadata.
It focuses on writing quality, not runtime security scanning.

use_cases.title: Refine a New Skill Draft
use_cases.description: Use the guidance to tighten descriptions, structure reference, and remove lines that do not change model behavior.

use_cases.title: Review Existing Skill Documentation
use_cases.description: Apply the glossary to spot duplication, weak completion criteria, unclear pointers, and bloated reference sections.

use_cases.title: Build a Shared Skill Vocabulary
use_cases.description: Use the glossary terms to align teams on invocation, context load, progressive disclosure, and pruning decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Skill Description
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one skill description.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-great-skills to review this skill description. Tell me whether it has clear triggers, a strong leading word, and unnecessary wording.

prompt_templates.title: Prune a Skill Draft
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic editing pass for an existing draft.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-great-skills to prune this skill draft. Identify duplication, sediment, no-op lines, and reference that should move behind a pointer.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Invocation Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate design choice for a skill set.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-great-skills to decide whether these skills should be model-invoked, user-invoked, or routed. Explain the context load and cognitive load tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Skill Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across multiple related skills.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-great-skills to audit this skill architecture. Check granularity, branches, progressive disclosure, completion criteria, and single sources of truth.

output_examples.input: Review my skill description for invocation quality.
output_examples.output: The description has a clear topic, but the trigger language repeats one branch three ways. Collapse the synonyms and keep the strongest leading word.

output_examples.input: Tell me whether this reference should stay in SKILL.md.
output_examples.output: Move it behind a context pointer if only one branch needs it. Keep it inline if every run needs the rule to behave correctly.

output_examples.input: Find failure modes in this skill draft.
output: Duplication: one behavior is explained in two sections.
Sprawl: the main skill file carries reference that can be disclosed.
No-op: one instruction repeats behavior the model already performs.

best_practices: Use the glossary terms when asking for reviews, so feedback is precise.
Review descriptions separately from body content, because they affect invocation.
Prune by meaning, not by sentence length, to preserve one source of truth.
anti_patterns: Treating every helpful note as content that belongs in SKILL.md.
Repeating the same trigger branch with different synonyms.
Splitting skills without a clear invocation, branch, or sequence reason.

faq.question: What does this skill help me write?
faq.answer: It helps you write clearer AI skills with better invocation, structure, reference placement, and pruning discipline.

faq.question: Does it generate complete skill packages?
faq.answer: No. It gives principles and vocabulary for improving skill instructions and related reference files.

faq.question: Can it help with Claude Code or Codex skills?
faq.answer: Yes. The ideas apply to Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and similar agent skill formats.

faq.question: What is the main design principle?
faq.answer: The main principle is predictability. The skill should make the agent follow the same process reliably.

faq.question: When should I use the glossary?
faq.answer: Use it when reviewing specific terms such as context load, progressive disclosure, duplication, or completion criterion.

faq.question: Is this a security scanning skill?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on writing and structure quality, not vulnerability detection or runtime behavior.
